result:
There is a quiet kind of arrogance in the way we measure time. We chop it into seconds, days, and decades, imposing a frantic rhythm onto a world that breathes in millennia. If you want to understand true patience, don't look at a clock; look at a mountain. Look at the slow, deliberate shrug of tectonic plates grinding against one another, pushing ancient stone skyward over eons.

We worry about tomorrow's deadlines or next year's ambitions, completely blind to the deep time operating beneath our feet. The vast ocean doesn't care about our plans for the next five years. It methodically gnaws at the coastline, turning granite cliffs into sand, grain by grain, century by century.

There is immense comfort in this geological indifference. Our mistakes, our heartbreaks, our fleeting triumphs; they are but a blink in the planet's long, unblinking eye. A human life is a mayfly dancing on the surface of a river, unaware of the ancient bedrock carved beneath.

Next time you feel overwhelmed by the frantic pace of your own brief existence, go outside and find the oldest tree you can. Press your palm against its rough bark. Feel the ancient rings hidden beneath the surface, each one a silent chronicle of a harsh winter survived and a warm summer embraced. Remember, the universe is in no real rush. The great mountains are still rising, the glaciers are still carving their valleys, and the earth will keep spinning long after our ticking watches have finally stopped.
